item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the few articles on-site to genuinely make me scared. Wonderful job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is possibly one of the best scps ive ever read +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really felt like the ramblings get old pretty fast, since they tend to repeat quite a lot. But that is literally my only complaint about this piece. I really enjoyed reading it and thought that it is mostly very well executed. An easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confused, did he make it back or not?

item.title: Oh my gosh
item.content:encoded:
I seriously don't care how old this SCP is.

This took me on a journey, dude! I still don't understand the anomaly itself too much, but that's totally fine. The story you're telling got me so good. Especially the ending! No idea if you will ever see this OZ Ouroboros, but thank you for this amazing SCP!
I tiny thing you might want to consider (no idea if anyone has said this yet): for the very last log (Anna's log) , maybe add who's speaking. Could be helpful, no?

Anyway, great thing. I love this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hated it
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The implication I always got was that the splatters were the half of his brain he'd already lost, and he's still stuck in the reality, now without the recorder or most of his body. I felt it was implied by how he talked about how his thumb was just staying in the 'air' after he pulled it off, so I sorta assumed all the parts that had fallen off him, and all the blood he'd lost, was floating around the recording panel. He talks about losing his ring, too, which was among the parts she found, so that's part of why I think the bits that fell through with the panel was all the stuff not attached to him. Of course… that's arguably worse for him, since he's even more stuck and alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GAHH this scp is so good and I already read it but I read it again and it made my head hurt. I think 3001 scp articles in my head is too much… but I must keep going

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think he lived, either. Judging by the last entry, he was only half a head left by the end. The splatters were his last remaining brain matter before he completely disintegrated. Although…what was he talking about, when he said that he had five more years which he would than re-stabilize? That doesn't make sense. Unfortunately, I think he's just plain gone, and the saddest part is knowing he might have been saved if only that newer technology could have been created and tested much sooner. What Robert went through is a special kind of hell that's kept me up at night for quite some time now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think he survived, was more than his biological material was all over the control panel, and she recognized the control panel and put two & two together…

Whereas she had thought he had died, she learned he suffered and "died" in a terrible manner

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wait, so did he survive the return (or what's left of him)?

Also, upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is undoubtedly the best skip I have ever read.
(+1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ho boy! Having reread it countless times this still holds up and remains my favorite SCP! Chilling! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. There is no canon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is not what the author is trying to say, please don't treat this as a fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This excellent video, re-enacts the events of this SCP. Seriously go watch it, this video made me greatly appreciate this SCP on a whole new level.

The Foundation: SCP 3001 - Red Reality (feat. GemoDawn)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For those who don’t get it, the void Robert was in was SCP-106’s pocket dimension, and Robert is SCP-106.

This is an SCP-106 origin story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I admire the way this scares me. It echoes a deep human fear. Alone, stuck indefinitely in the dark, with no way out and no way to die? Watching Dr. Scranton's descent into madness really sticks with me, because it terrifies me. And yet, the "story" slowly turns into something solely terrifying into something also emotional and moving… almost a tragic love story. A twisted, horrifying one at that. Certainly deserving of a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely my favorite SCP. This isn’t just a file with an anomalous object, it’s a story with a message that made me almost cry. I read some of this to my friends who at first were like, “eh not right now”. But when I started reading they were invested in the story, asking questions, “Wait why is his body breaking apart again? What do Hume levels do again?” And begin to analyze the story. I’ve even drawn art for 3001. The story is just so amazing, there’s no way it couldn’t be my favorite SCP. +1 without a doubt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why did you do this to me? WHY?!?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of that Spongebob episode where Squidward is transported to the Alone Reality

definitely worth a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorites on this entire site. I wish I could give it multiple +1s.

Sort-of a soundtrack for it: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tfxrqEDM7hQ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking hell man, this with the new -106 is just… god…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my absolute favourite SCPs. The first time I read it, the dread stayed with me for weeks. Bravo.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made me depressed
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is without a doubt my favorite article on this entire site. I just can't stop coming back to read it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoroughly entertaining all throughout, and also emotionally provocative. Robert's anguish over his lost love and existential nightmare was truly visceral.

I think the part that freaks me out the most is that he himself underwent "waves." Namely, he shifted from desperate for survival to suicidal and back to desperate for suicidal once again. Tragic how an ounce of hope can reinvigorate the horror of one's situation.

It would seem that those who write SCPs have found a superb use for the universe: an Internet repository of Twilight Zone episodes… or something close to it. Seems like a fitting work-a-round in regards to the otherwise "sterile" surface of SCP files on the whole.

Now, I'm a huge idiot, but what happened to Anna at the end? Did she just faint or was there something far more sinister in the "thudding sound"?

One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.
One year, six months, eight days.

It took a while, but congratulations on breaking 1000!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are very few SCPs where I need to stare at the screen and process the entire SCP and what has just happened.

Atonement is one of them.

This one is another, as well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, he supposedly died while creating a reality-bending device, got trapped in a pocket dimension, mutated, and went insane?
So he's Omega from Doctor Who?
I feel like so many elements were slightly taken from Omega, but either way, it was pretty good, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely heartwrenching, and Dr. Lang's breakdown at the very end sealed in my +1. The ending especially was a mindscrew for me.

It's grey, his grey, oh, god, where's the other…?

These lines at the end of the audio logs make it sound like Scranton DID make it back, just not in one piece, and the "so much blood" was all that was left of him. However, the end of the object description implies that he's still out there in the Red Reality. Due to the fact that the void has so little reality, it's really hard to tell whether Scranton is still trapped in the void, surviving only due to his sheer unreality and retaining duplicates of his lost body parts because he believes hard enough in them, OR if he was just liquefied on the way home because he kept pulling himself apart. It's creepy either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A year late, but he does. He moves just by thinking about it, but There's just so little reality that there's nothing else he can do with it, and by the time he'd want to, such as when he started to discorperate, his hume field would have started to equalize. In effect, as the most powerful reality gender in the dimension, all he could really do was hold himself together for a few years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome back! I'm excited for you - there's a lot to discover in series 4.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been away from the mainsite for quite a long time now and decided I'd break the fast with this one. And holy fucking shit I was not in any way disappointed.

This is honestly an incredible scip that somehow, somehow balances 'official document' and 'gripping story' into one piece. And does so incredibly well.

+1, and if it were possible I'd upvote it twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just so you know that I keep coming back to this, even after more than a year. This struck really hard on me.

Also, I have this beauty as my phone's wallpaper, thanks to SunnyClockwork.

+ Show Image
- Hide

Please place your forum images in collapsibles. — Accel

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An amazing heart breaking entity that troubles my ideas on dark matter,what if dark matter was another reality that effected our own,what if we are in between two realities? Amazing and emotionally stunning scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Speaking in my capacity as site criticism staff, I am going to tell you that this feedback is unhelpful. Try to be more specific and give the author examples of things they can change instead of just stating your opinion. Thank you!

EDIT: I would also add that this kind of behavior is unacceptable and advise you to be less belligerent in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'story bit' is appropriately framed in a reasonable way that's perfectly suitable as files appended to in-universe documentation. Perhaps you're behind in reading material, but things like that are fine to include in SCPs and have been for years now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trash, that's all I have to say. You should move the story bit into a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first ever Scp that got me teary eyed. Solid +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There’s only a few entries on this site where I need to just sit back and process everything for a minute. Let it all sink in. +1 goddamn you deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is absolutly beautiful. I really love articles that use scp formating not only to tell about a scary spooky item/event/creature but that also tell a story. This SCP has a heart breaking story. You have isolation, breaking into madness, lliterally dying in the slowest way possible having reality shattered and a love story to relate to.
This is the first article of this wiki that made me cry. Making something that can give such strong emotions to people is not common, congratulations.

+1 and one of my favorite SCPs now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think any other article on this site has impacted me as emotionally as this one. You can sense the sheer desperation and despair for Scantron. All he wanted was to just see his wife again. But the only thing he had left was little red…

+1. I hope to see more like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I read through this again yesterday, and, uh…

I don't know how you did it. I was sad the first time but now I can't fucking sleep because of it. I know it sounds melodramatic but I literally lost sleep over this thing. It's just so fucking sad. Your article struck me like the fucking eel that should have been second place compared to this. Absolutely tremendous. Because of this, I deem this my new ultimate favorite SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't Scranton only be 38 years old if he was born September 1961 and got wormholed January 2000 (38 years and about 4 months)? He shouldn't be forgetting such basic information about himself in the first log. :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I literally cried at the end..

It was so poetic and sad, just to imagine how much time Robert spent at this parallel world…

Good job dude <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What gives you the right to break my heart like that?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this article; the relation to actual scientific theories helped really bring out the science aspects of the Foundation, and the audio logs showed the scientist's descent into madness. While I felt that things got a little repetitive at times, overall this was a great SCP article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't comment on this one because it's obviously so popular so I felt there was no need. But when I enjoy something so much, I feel the need to express it. Getting into that cerebral type of horror, making you think that maybe your brain couldn't comprehend what it's being told, it was drew me here in the first place. This is terrible and awful in all the right ways. +1 and added to my favorites list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well no because it was a pocket nondimension that was entered accidentally by the foundation not made. i can see what you mean though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this have the Foundation Made tag?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
most of the time when i finish reading a SCP I'm just confused as to how something like that could come into existence or why it did. this i just felt sorry for that guy. i know it isn't real but Jesus is that dialog realistic.
+1 with insane enthusiasm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scranton's remains and the LSS module.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, what exactly came out during the last log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rip Scantron

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's 4 AM where I currently am, and I can't sleep. My PC broke so I can't play games, so I resorted to reading SCP, picking up where I left off, well, probably 2 years ago. I haven't digested more than 100 SCPs, hell, I've only read maybe 10 Tales. I think I'm a lucky clicker. Given the limited amount of entries I've read, almost all of them give me a sensation as if I just discovered something truly NEW, like my mind just hit that Refresh button, or experiencing something great for the first time again. I think this is the only place where that can occur to me now.

+ show block
– hide block

I remember feeling like my throat is being seized when I read 231. It's impending doom, everything's down to the last straw that mustn't be broken, but will be broken. To buy time, they have to do something so horrible, it's absolutely unspeakable. People don't even talk about 231, they ask over and over "what is 105-Montauk". The tales are mainly about 105-Montauk. No one dies, it is indicated so, but it's so much worse than the loss of lives that no one can know.

I remember feeling utterly hopeless when reading 3125 and its attached Tales. The insanity of an alien reality was so evident and graphic in the behavior and mannerism of affected personnel. The horrors of antimemetic SCPs. The suspense I had when Kim got chased by Grey, the infectious despair of an exhausted Wheeler. The untold truth that the Division was founded god knows how many times and no one remembers it. The almighty O5s are rendered helpless. A war they cannot win because they don't even know they're fighting.

3001?
It doesn't have anything in it. It doesn't have that doomsday horror, it isn't a malicious entity that's unstoppable and will eventually wipe us out. It's just nothing, infinite nothingness, the meaningless overlooked non-space that is likely to be discovered only once by man, and it'll sit there forever, undisturbed. It claimed only one victim, and is likely to remain so indefinitely. A housefly probably killed more people through food contamination than that.

The suffering of Scranton resonates so much more than an LK-class-destruction-of-the-word. It's unimaginable, almost alien. The curse of nigh immortality is chilling. You just can't die, but you still feel everything. You feel your Hume zipping away, your body falling apart, your mind descending into insanity, yet the end is never there. You keep walking, but you never hit a wall. You keep going up, then down, and every imaginable direction, but you never reach any destination. The little red light is everything you have aside from your rotting body. As I scroll through Scranton's ramblings, Red suddenly became my friend as well. It'd have been a different journey without Red, it'd just be Scranton dying alone and no one can know anything about him anymore. But Red set the story on a different course. The entirety of Scranton's suffering traveled from irreality back to his wife, along with a physical confirmation that he is beyond saving. It's no longer a blacked out, redacted, expunged, or corrupted piece of data that indicates "something really bad happened that we writers'd like to leave to your imagination, bunch of personnel died horribly, SCP's dangerous, gotta lock it down". It's all there. Scranton didn't die because of [EXPUNGED] and [REDACTED], he simply rotted away through Hume diffusion in a low Hume space, at the same time he attempted multiple bodily mutilations. The body horror isn't the sole selling point, it's the fact that Scranton didn't die from it, and is most likely still floating in 3001 with half his brain and whatever that's left of his body and sanity, now that Red is gone. I believe that near the end he couldn't even produce actual tears now, and when his vocal cords get too damaged, he's gonna have to talk in thoughts, he won't hear anything anymore, not his own voice or Red's time announcement. And Red won't be there to deliver unto us the final part of his suffering, only that it resonates in our imagination, like when we endlessly try to envision 105-Montauk or a containment procedure for 3125.

No one knows what happened to preys inside 106's pocket dimension. Or personnel dragged through that Red Pond. Or whether people were still alive the entire time after 004's activation. Or people who got conceptually blown up by an antimemetic bomb and reduced to mere ghosts. Did they suffer the same thing as Scranton? Did they feel the same pain as his? What thoughts ran through their head? One thing for certain, Scranton might still be alive right now, alone, without even a monster or something scary to toy with him. He's just there, all by himself, rotting, counting down to the moment he ends. Maybe when the Heat Death approaches, we'll all be doing the same.


Please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

SCPs have always been a source of great intrigue. They've always filled me with a sense of wonder, horror, astonishment, surprise, you name it. But 3001 is the first one that made me teared up. Rest in peace Scranton, if you ever will.

+1

item.title: Re: +700
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot put into words how uncomfortable this SCP made me.

I love it.
My name is Anna, and I hate how much I love this. Take my upvote and your +700, both richly deserved.
